On 1 April 2009 **structural changes to local government in England** took place which reformed the local government of seven [non-metropolitan counties](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_county "Non-metropolitan county"): [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), and [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire"). In each case the government of the county was changed from a two-tier to a [unitary](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") system, with Bedfordshire and Cheshire being divided into two new unitary authorities.

[Elections to the new authorities](/wiki/2008_United_Kingdom_local_elections "2008 United Kingdom local elections") took place on 1 May 2008 for some of the new authorities, which took up their powers on 1 April 2009. In the remaining authorities elections took place two months later on 4 June 2009.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Following the passing of the Local Government Act 1992, the [Local Government Commission for England](/wiki/Local_Government_Commission_for_England_(1992) "Local Government Commission for England (1992)") recommended a number of unitary authorities to be created in England during the 1990s. The changes that were implemented meant that much of the country continued to have a two-tier arrangement of local government.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Further proposals for [unitary authorities in northern England](/wiki/2004_Northern_England_devolution_referendums "2004 Northern England devolution referendums"), tied to the option of regional devolution, were put to a referendum in 2004 and were rejected. In 2006 the white paper *Strong and Prosperous Communities* invited local authorities in England to submit their own consensus-based proposals for unitary authority arrangements, to be submitted before 25 January 2007. Selected submissions went to a public consultation from March until June, with successful proposals announced in July. Legislative requirements for implementation were in place following the enactment of the [Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007](/wiki/Local_Government_and_Public_Involvement_in_Health_Act_2007 "Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007").

## Proposed changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Proposed changes")]

### Early responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early responses")]

Various local councils indicated their wish to seek unitary authority status. One town and three cities were overlooked by the 1990s review: [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich_(borough) "Ipswich (borough)"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") and [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter")[[2]](#cite_note-2) proposed unitary status on their present boundaries, and commissioned a report jointly to press their case.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Norwich announced its intention to respond to the invitation,[[4]](#cite_note-4) as did Ipswich and Exeter.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) In [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), [Preston](/wiki/City_of_Preston,_Lancashire "City of Preston, Lancashire") and [South Ribble](/wiki/South_Ribble "South Ribble") desired to form a single unitary authority although Preston bid for it alone.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The [City of Lancaster](/wiki/City_of_Lancaster "City of Lancaster") considered seeking unitary status on its present boundaries (having supported a merger with [South Lakeland](/wiki/South_Lakeland "South Lakeland") and [Barrow-in-Furness](/wiki/Barrow-in-Furness_(borough) "Barrow-in-Furness (borough)") to form a [Morecambe Bay](/wiki/Morecambe_Bay "Morecambe Bay") unitary authority during the referendums review).[[9]](#cite_note-9) Blackpool advocated a merger with the [Fylde](/wiki/Fylde_(borough) "Fylde (borough)") and [Wyre](/wiki/Borough_of_Wyre "Borough of Wyre") districts, which they did not support.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Pendle](/wiki/Borough_of_Pendle "Borough of Pendle") and [Burnley](/wiki/Borough_of_Burnley "Borough of Burnley") also tried to form a unitary authority with [Rossendale](/wiki/Borough_of_Rossendale "Borough of Rossendale"); however Rossendale rejected this.

The [Local Government Chronicle](/wiki/Local_Government_Chronicle "Local Government Chronicle") suggested that the non-metropolitan counties of [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria") and [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") would fit the government's criteria, and that the government would be unlikely to favour carving out unitary authorities from existing two-tier counties.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Shropshire County Council, as well as two of the five districts of Shropshire, stated their wish for a move to unitary status.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) The issue was considered in Durham and Cumbria[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) and the idea of a North Cumbria authority covering the Eden, Copeland, Carlisle and Allerdale districts had some support.[[16]](#cite_note-16) The issue was also considered in Northumberland, with the county council in favour of one Northumberland unitary authority. Alan Beith, the MP for [Berwick](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed") at the far north of Northumberland, suggested instead a three unitary solution, with authorities for the largely rural north and south-west, and an authority for the urban south-east ([Wansbeck](/wiki/Wansbeck_District "Wansbeck District") and [Blyth Valley](/wiki/Blyth_Valley "Blyth Valley")).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Consultation period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Consultation period")]

On 26 January 2007, the government confirmed that 26 proposals for unitary authorities had been received.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Various county councils proposed they should become unitary authorities: [Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire"), [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria"), [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire"), [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") and [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire"). Districts seeking to become unitary authorities on their own were [Bedford](/wiki/Borough_of_Bedford "Borough of Bedford"), [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter"), [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich "Ipswich"), [Lancaster](/wiki/City_of_Lancaster "City of Lancaster"), [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), [Preston](/wiki/City_of_Preston,_Lancashire "City of Preston, Lancashire"). [Pendle](/wiki/Borough_of_Pendle "Borough of Pendle") and [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_(borough) "Burnley (borough)") proposed merging as a unitary authority. On 27 March 2007, the government announced that the proposals by Bedfordshire, Bedford, Cornwall, Cheshire, Cumbria, Durham, Exeter, Ipswich, North Yorkshire, Norwich, Shropshire, Somerset and Wiltshire to become unitary authorities would go into the next phase, as would the proposal of Chester for a two-unitary authority Cheshire and by the districts of Northumberland for a two-unitary Northumberland.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On 25 July 2007 it was announced that the unitary proposals by [Cumbria](/wiki/Cumbria "Cumbria"), [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") and [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset")[[20]](#cite_note-Unitary_authority_plan_rejected-20) had been rejected.[[21]](#cite_note-21) On 5 December 2007 it was explained that the unitary proposals by [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter"), and [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich "Ipswich"), like those of [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") (which were referred for review on 25 July 2007) did not meet the requirements to proceed; but may be reconsidered after the [Boundary Committee](/wiki/Boundary_Committee_for_England "Boundary Committee for England") has conducted a review of local government structures across the whole of the two-tier counties they are currently a part of.

A legal challenge was made by [Shrewsbury and Atcham Borough Council](/wiki/Shrewsbury_and_Atcham "Shrewsbury and Atcham") and by [Congleton Borough Council](/wiki/Congleton "Congleton") to the government's decisions, on the basis (amongst other things) that the reviews were made without statutory authority. The [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_England_and_Wales "High Court of England and Wales") and then on 4 March 2008 the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_England_and_Wales "Court of Appeal of England and Wales") rejected the challenge, finding that the review had been legalised retrospectively.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Successful proposals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Successful proposals")]

On 27 March 2007 Local Government Minister [Phil Woolas](/wiki/Phil_Woolas "Phil Woolas") announced that 16 councils bidding for unitary status had been shortlisted to go forward for public consultation.[[26]](#cite_note-26) On 25 July 2007 Woolas' successor [John Healey](/wiki/John_Healey_(politician) "John Healey (politician)") announced that nine proposals would proceed, subject to the approval of [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in the [Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007](/wiki/Local_Government_and_Public_Involvement_in_Health_Act_2007 "Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007").[[27]](#cite_note-27) A further announcement on 5 December indicated that only the five county level proposals ([Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"), [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland"), [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire"), and [Wiltshire](/wiki/Wiltshire "Wiltshire")) would proceed, while further consideration was to be given to the various proposals submitted for two more county areas ([Bedfordshire](/wiki/Bedfordshire "Bedfordshire") and [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire")).[[28]](#cite_note-28) On 18 December it was confirmed that the two-authority plan for [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire "Cheshire") would proceed.[[29]](#cite_note-29) On 6 March 2008 it was confirmed that proposals for [Bedford](/wiki/Borough_of_Bedford "Borough of Bedford") Borough Council to become a unitary authority on its existing boundaries, and for Mid Bedfordshire District Council and South Bedfordshire District Council to merge to create a new [Central Bedfordshire](/wiki/Central_Bedfordshire "Central Bedfordshire") authority would be implemented.

## Further changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Further changes")]

The [Boundary Committee for England](/wiki/Boundary_Committee_for_England "Boundary Committee for England") has completed its structural reviews for [Norfolk](/wiki/Norfolk "Norfolk"), [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk") and [Devon](/wiki/Devon "Devon").[[32]](#cite_note-electoralcommission.org.uk-32)

Initial draft proposals were published in July 2008. The committee was expected to make final recommendations by 31 December 2008, but these were delayed, due to legal challenges;[[33]](#cite_note-33) and because they didn't assess the potential new authorities in proposals which would have split up the counties between two or more unitaries in aggregate. As a result, a further set of draft proposals were published in March 2009:

### Draft proposals for Devon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Draft proposals for Devon")]

The further draft recommendations propose two unitary options:

* A Devon unitary authority comprising the existing county of Devon, with no changes to [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth "Plymouth") and [Torbay](/wiki/Torbay "Torbay").
* An [Exeter](/wiki/Exeter "Exeter") and [Exmouth](/wiki/Exmouth "Exmouth") unitary authority, and a unitary authority covering the remainder of Devon, with no changes to Plymouth and Torbay.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Draft proposals for Norfolk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Draft proposals for Norfolk")]

The further draft recommendations propose two unitary options:

* A single Norfolk unitary authority comprising the existing county of Norfolk.
* A two authority structure with greater [Norwich](/wiki/Norwich "Norwich") unitary authority and a rural Norfolk unitary authority for the rest of the county.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Draft proposals for Suffolk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Draft proposals for Suffolk")]

The further draft recommendations propose two unitary options:

* A single Suffolk unitary authority comprising the existing county of Suffolk.
* A two authority structure with a North Haven [[36]](#cite_note-36) unitary authority consisting of [Ipswich](/wiki/Ipswich "Ipswich") and [Felixstowe](/wiki/Felixstowe "Felixstowe"), and a rural Suffolk unitary authority for the rest of the county.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Final recommendations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Final recommendations")]

Final recommendations were then due in July 2009,[[32]](#cite_note-electoralcommission.org.uk-32) but a successful legal challenge from three District Councils in Suffolk wanting a three unitary model for Suffolk to be considered by the committee resulted in a further delay. The Boundary Committee appealed this decision in favour of Forest Heath, St Edmundsbury & Waveney Councils in October 2009; in December (2009) they found out their appeal had been successful.

The Boundary Committee has now submitted its final recommendations to the Secretary of State. In all three counties the committee has stated that its preferred option is for a countywide unitary covering the whole of the existing two-tier area to be implemented. In addition in relation to Suffolk they have stated they have no major reservations about the two unitary pattern of North Haven & Rural Suffolk being adopted.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Interested parties had until 19 January 2010 to make their representations to the Secretary of State.[[32]](#cite_note-electoralcommission.org.uk-32)

### Decision on reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Decision on reform")]

On 10 February 2010, [Rosie Winterton](/wiki/Rosie_Winterton "Rosie Winterton"), Minister of State for Local Government, announced the proposed final decisions on the unitary proposals:[[24]](#cite_note-clg2010-24)

* Norwich to become a unitary authority, the remainder of Norfolk to retain two-tier structure due to lack of support for change.[[24]](#cite_note-clg2010-24)
* Exeter to become a unitary authority, the remainder of Devon to retain two-tier structure, again due to lack of support locally. The boundaries of Exeter are to remain unchanged.[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* Suffolk to be divided into unitary authority areas, the boundaries of which will be decided by a countywide convention.[[24]](#cite_note-clg2010-24)

The changes in Devon/Exeter and Norfolk/Norwich were to come into effect in April 2011, subject to judicial review. Draft structural changes orders were approved by the House of Lords on 22 March 2010,[[40]](#cite_note-40) though the Lords took the unusual step of formally noting its regret.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The amendments did not alter the draft orders or affect the legal validity of the Lords approval. The House of Commons approved the draft orders on 23 March.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The final Exeter and Norwich orders were made on 24 March following a Government statement responding to the Lords vote.[[43]](#cite_note-43) However, the orders were quashed in a judicial review[[44]](#cite_note-44) causing elections to be held in Exeter and Norwich in September 2010.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Cancellation of further structural changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_structural_changes_to_local_government_in_England&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Cancellation of further structural changes")]

Main article: [Local Government Act 2010](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_2010 "Local Government Act 2010")

The [Queen's Speech](/wiki/Queen%27s_Speech "Queen's Speech") at the May 2010 [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") included a Local Government Bill to revoke structural change orders that would have established Exeter and Norwich as unitary authorities and prevent the implementation of the Suffolk unitary proposals.[[46]](#cite_note-46) The bill was introduced in the House of Lords on 27 May 2010.[[47]](#cite_note-47)
